papers.title: Construction of higher-order metric fluctuation terms in spacetime symmetry-breaking effective field theory
papers.abstract: We examine the basic conservation laws for diffeomorphism symmetry in the context of spontaneous diffeomorphism and local Lorentz-symmetry breaking. The conservation laws are used as constraints on a generic series of terms in an expansion around a flat background. We find all such terms for a two-tensor coupling to cubic order in the metric and tensor field fluctuations. The results are presented in a form that can be used for phenomenological calculations. One key result is that if we preserve the underlying diffeomorphism symmetry in a spontaneous-symmetry breaking scenario, one cannot decouple the two-tensor fluctuations from the metric fluctuations at the level of the action, except in special cases of the quadratic actions.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's research idea focuses on using graph neural networks and DFT data to predict elastic tensor components for alloys, explicitly encoding crystallographic symmetry. The paper, in contrast, investigates higher-order metric fluctuation terms in spacetime symmetry-breaking effective field theory, specifically concerning diffeomorphism and local Lorentz symmetry. These are fundamentally different research problems in distinct scientific domains (materials science/machine learning vs. theoretical physics/gravitation). | Method: The user's research idea proposes using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with DFT-computed property datasets to predict elastic tensor components by encoding crystallographic symmetry constraints into message-passing rules. The evaluated paper, on the other hand, focuses on effective field theory, spontaneous symmetry breaking, and the construction of higher-order metric fluctuation terms in the context of spacetime symmetries (diffeomorphism and local Lorentz symmetry). There is no methodological overlap; the paper does not mention GNNs, machine learning, DFT, or material property prediction. | Domain: The user's research idea is in materials science, specifically predicting elastic tensor components for alloys using graph neural networks and DFT data. The paper, however, is in theoretical physics, focusing on spacetime symmetry-breaking effective field theory and gravitational theories. The domains, methodologies, and specific contributions are entirely unrelated.

papers.title: A Neural Network Perturbation Theory Based on the Born Series
papers.abstract: Deep Learning using the eponymous deep neural networks (DNNs) has become an attractive approach towards various data-based problems of theoretical physics in the past decade. There has been a clear trend to deeper architectures containing increasingly more powerful and involved layers. Contrarily, Taylor coefficients of DNNs still appear mainly in the light of interpretability studies, where they are computed at most to first order. However, especially in theoretical physics numerous problems benefit from accessing higher orders, as well. This gap motivates a general formulation of neural network (NN) Taylor expansions. Restricting our analysis to multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and introducing quantities we refer to as propagators and vertices, both depending on the MLP's weights and biases, we establish a graph-theoretical approach. Similarly to Feynman rules in quantum field theories, we can systematically assign diagrams containing propagators and vertices to the corresponding partial derivative. Examining this approach for S-wave scattering lengths of shallow potentials, we observe NNs to adapt their derivatives mainly to the leading order of the target function's Taylor expansion. To circumvent this problem, we propose an iterative NN perturbation theory. During each iteration we eliminate the leading order, such that the next-to-leading order can be faithfully learned during the subsequent iteration. After performing two iterations, we find that the first- and second-order Born terms are correctly adapted during the respective iterations. Finally, we combine both results to find a proxy that acts as a machine-learned second-order Born approximation.

These are fundamentally different research problems and domains. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper utilize neural networks, placing them in the same broad category of deep learning. This is distinct from the user's idea of training a graph neural network to predict elastic tensor components for alloys by encoding crystallographic symmetry constraints into message-passing rules.

papers.title: Graph Neural Networks for Transmission Grid Topology Control: Busbar Information Asymmetry and Heterogeneous Representations
papers.abstract: Factors such as the proliferation of renewable energy and electrification contribute to grid congestion as a pressing problem. Topology control is an appealing method for relieving congestion, but traditional approaches for topology discovery have proven too slow for practical application. Recent research has focused on machine learning (ML) as an efficient alternative. Graph neural networks (GNNs) are particularly well-suited for topology control applications due to their ability to model the graph structure of power grids. This study investigates the effect of the graph representation on GNN effectiveness for topology control. We identify the busbar information asymmetry problem inherent to the popular homogeneous graph representation. We propose a heterogeneous graph representation that resolves this problem. We apply GNNs with both representations and a fully connected neural network (FCNN) baseline on an imitation learning task. The models are evaluated by classification accuracy and grid operation ability. We find that heterogeneous GNNs perform best on in-distribution network configurations, followed by FCNNs, and lastly, homogeneous GNNs. We also find that both GNN types generalize better to out-of-distribution network configurations than FCNNs.

These are entirely different application domains and specific technical contributions.

papers.title: Corrections to Wigner-Eckart Relations by Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking
papers.abstract: The matrix elements of operators transforming as irreducible representations of an unbroken symmetry group $G$ are governed by the well-known Wigner-Eckart relations. In the case of infinitely-extended systems, with $G$ spontaneously broken, we prove that the corrections to such relations are provided by symmetry breaking Ward identities, and simply reduce to a tadpole term involving Goldstone bosons. The analysis extends to the case in which an explicit symmetry breaking term is present in the Hamiltonian, with the tadpole term now involving pseudo Goldstone bosons. An explicit example is discussed, illustrating the two cases.

papers.title: Undercooling growth and magnetic characterization of ferromagnetic shape memory alloy Ni2FeGa single crystals
papers.abstract: Ni2FeGa single crystals have been grown in undercooling conditions provided by a glass-purification method. It has been found that trace amounts of gamma phase embededin the single crystalline matrix preferentially orients in the <100> orientation along the growth direction. This gamma phase generates directional residual stress and results in an anisotropic two-way shape memory effect. Large strains of -2.5% in the [001] and 1.5% in the [010] directions have been observed. This trace gamma phase also improves the ductility of the material and thus the crystals could be plastically deformed at room temperature in the parent phase. The <110> and <111> orientations in Ni2FeGa alloy were identified as the easy and hard magnetization directions, respectively, in the parent phase by using low field M-T measurements.

existing_examples.name: Materials Project
existing_examples.description: A large database of DFT-computed materials properties, which could serve as a dataset for such a model.

existing_examples.name: Matminer/Pymatgen
existing_examples.description: Software libraries for materials data mining and analysis, including symmetry operations and crystal structure manipulation.

existing_examples.name: MEGNet (Materials Graph Network)
existing_examples.description: A prominent graph neural network architecture for materials science that predicts properties from crystal structures, trained on DFT data. It learns symmetry implicitly.

existing_examples.name: ALIGNN (Atomistic Line Graph Neural Network)
existing_examples.description: Another GNN for materials property prediction, also trained on DFT data, and aims to capture structural information, but typically learns symmetry implicitly.

existing_examples.name: E(3)-equivariant GNNs (e.g., NequIP, Allegro)
existing_examples.description: These GNNs are designed to be invariant/equivariant to 3D rotations, translations, and reflections, which is a form of encoding symmetry. However, they typically focus on point group symmetries for atomic environments, not necessarily the full crystallographic space group symmetry for macroscopic tensor properties in the message-passing rules directly for elastic tensors.

reality_check.assessment: The core idea of using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to predict materials properties, including elastic tensors, from DFT-computed datasets is a well-established and active research area. Many GNN architectures (e.g., MEGNet, ALIGNN) already exist for this purpose. The key novelty here lies in 'encoding crystallographic symmetry constraints directly into the message-passing aggregation rules, rather than learning symmetry implicitly.' While E(3)-equivariant GNNs incorporate some forms of symmetry, the explicit encoding of *crystallographic space group symmetry* into the *message-passing aggregation rules* for *elastic tensor components* is a specific and potentially novel approach that goes beyond typical implicit learning or general E(3) equivariance. This specific method of direct encoding for this particular application (elastic tensors of alloys) appears to be a distinct research direction, even if the broader components (GNNs, DFT data, elastic tensor prediction) are not new.
novelty_aspects: Direct encoding of crystallographic symmetry constraints into GNN message-passing aggregation rules.
Application of this specific symmetry-aware GNN design to predict elastic tensor components for alloys.
reality_check.recommendation: This idea has a strong foundation in existing research but proposes a specific, potentially novel methodological improvement. Focus your research on clearly defining how the crystallographic symmetry constraints are *directly encoded* into the message-passing rules and demonstrate its advantages (e.g., improved accuracy, data efficiency, interpretability) compared to existing GNNs that learn symmetry implicitly or use general E(3) equivariance. A thorough literature review on symmetry-aware GNNs in materials science, particularly those dealing with tensor properties, would be crucial.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Neural Network Perturbation Theory Based on the Born Series
matched_sections.heading: THE BORN SERIES AS A TAYLOR SERIES
matched_sections.text_snippet: At this point the following questions arise: What conditions must the given architecture satisfy such that loss minimization during training also causes the partial derivatives of the MLP for any given order to approximate the corresponding derivatives of the target function? Or asked differently: If we are given a raw data set and do not know the analytical representation of the target function, how can we be sure that its analytical structure is reproduced by the trained NN?
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea focuses on encoding 'crystallographic symmetry constraints directly into the message-passing aggregation rules, rather than learning symmetry implicitly'. This relates to the paper's broader concern about how NNs learn and reproduce the 'analytical structure' of target functions, particularly in how they adapt to underlying physical theories (like the Born series). While the specific problem (symmetry constraints vs. Born series) differs, both are concerned with how NNs handle underlying physical principles.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Neural Network Perturbation Theory Based on the Born Series
matched_sections.heading: DISCUSSION AND OUTLOOK
matched_sections.text_snippet: Note that the obvious next step of this analysis would be the interpretation of the constructed proxy in the space of all sampled potentials. In doing so, we would just gather a post-hoc interpretation, based on approximations and thus deviations from actual scattering lengths. In recent years there have been many efforts to close the gap between prediction and interpretation by ad-hoc interpretation methods. These exemplarily involve training NNs whose architectures are either intrinsically interpretable or can be brought in an interpretable representation, see Ref. [20].

matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Graph neural networks (GNNs) [23] have recently recieved much attention, both from the machine learning and power engineering community. Deep learning models have traditionally been designed for tabular, sequential, or grid-like data. Consequently, ML methods for power grid operation have historically not or minimally exploited the network structure of power grids. This is a major limitation as power grid topologies are dynamic and frequently change, for example due to maintenance. Graph neural networks encode network structure into their representation and are therefore well-suited for power grid modeling.
